raw_response:
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement is prescribing a spiritual practice and articulating a specific prayer intention, functioning as an instructional or aspirational guideline for personal spiritual development.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "There exists a God (or a higher power) that can be contacted.",
    "Prayer and meditation are effective means to improve conscious contact with that God.",
    "It is possible to know God's will.",
    "It is possible to receive power to carry out God's will.",
    "The individual has a capacity for conscious contact with God that can be improved.",
    "The concept of God is subject to personal interpretation ('as we understood Him')."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent: it presents a logical sequence of means (prayer and meditation), goal (improved conscious contact with God), and specific prayer content (knowledge of God's will and power to execute it). The inclusion of 'as we understood Him' allows for theological flexibility, preventing internal contradiction across differing definitions of God.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The user believes in a personal or relational God (or higher power) with whom conscious contact is possible and meaningful.",
    "The user accepts that prayer and meditation can facilitate such contact.",
    "The user desires guidance and empowerment beyond their own will.",
    "The user is open to the concept of a divine will that is knowable and good."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "The statement is presented as universally applicable to all people regardless of belief system.",
    "It is interpreted as requiring a specific theistic conception of God, excluding atheistic or non-theistic spiritual practices.",
    "It implies that one's own will is always inferior or that personal agency should be surrendered uncritically.",
    "It is used to justify passivity or avoidance of personal responsibility by claiming 'I am waiting for God's will.'",
    "It is understood to guarantee that prayer will lead to clear knowledge of God's will, which may not be experienced by all practitioners."
  ]
}

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement is prescribing a spiritual practice and articulating a specific prayer intention, functioning as an instructional or aspirational guideline for personal spiritual development.
implicit_claims: There exists a God (or a higher power) that can be contacted.
Prayer and meditation are effective means to improve conscious contact with that God.
It is possible to know God's will.
It is possible to receive power to carry out God's will.
The individual has a capacity for conscious contact with God that can be improved.
The concept of God is subject to personal interpretation ('as we understood Him').
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent: it presents a logical sequence of means (prayer and meditation), goal (improved conscious contact with God), and specific prayer content (knowledge of God's will and power to execute it). The inclusion of 'as we understood Him' allows for theological flexibility, preventing internal contradiction across differing definitions of God.
conditions_for_usefulness: The user believes in a personal or relational God (or higher power) with whom conscious contact is possible and meaningful.
The user accepts that prayer and meditation can facilitate such contact.
The user desires guidance and empowerment beyond their own will.
The user is open to the concept of a divine will that is knowable and good.
conditions_for_misleading: The statement is presented as universally applicable to all people regardless of belief system.
It is interpreted as requiring a specific theistic conception of God, excluding atheistic or non-theistic spiritual practices.
It implies that one's own will is always inferior or that personal agency should be surrendered uncritically.
It is used to justify passivity or avoidance of personal responsibility by claiming 'I am waiting for God's will.'
It is understood to guarantee that prayer will lead to clear knowledge of God's will, which may not be experienced by all practitioners.
